title: NOC 94122 – Paper converting machine operators | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, as Canada intensifies its transition toward sustainable, fiber-based packaging to replace single-use plastics, Paper Converting Machine Operators (NOC 94122) have become indispensable to the manufacturing supply chain. These operators run the high-speed machinery that transforms rolls of paper and cardboard into essential products like biodegradable containers, corrugated boxes, and eco-friendly paper bags. With a strong risk of labor shortage projected through 2033, the industry is increasingly looking toward international talent to manage modern, automated production lines.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Machine Operation: Running machines that cut, fold, glue, or clip paper and cardboard to create boxes, cartons, and paper bags.
Specialized Forming: Operating machinery that presses paper to form drinking cups, plates, and other specialized containers.
Setup & Calibration: Setting up machines according to job specifications and adjusting for different paper weights and lengths.
Maintenance: Cleaning, lubricating, and performing routine preventative maintenance on high-speed converting equipment.
2026 Update: Monitoring automated quality-control sensors that detect alignment and glue-consistency issues in real-time.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is typically required by larger employers.
Experience: Previous experience as a labourer within a manufacturing environment is often an asset but not always required.
Training: Several weeks of on-the-job training are standard for most entry-level operator positions.
Skills: Strong mechanical aptitude, attention to detail, and the ability to work in a fast-paced, sometimes noisy, industrial environment.
job_outlook: Nationally, the number of job openings is expected to total 2,500, while the number of job seekers is projected at only 600, indicating a significant opportunity for international workers. As traditional paper use declines, the expansion into specialized industrial and consumer packaging is creating a high-stability niche for skilled converters.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is driven by the global surge in e-commerce (packaging) and sustainable retail:
British Columbia: Strong demand. Driven by provincial plastic-reduction mandates increasing the need for paper-based alternatives.
Quebec: Moderate to Good. A significant portion of Canada's paper manufacturing is concentrated here, with fair prospects for new operators.
Ontario (GTA): Stable demand. While some sectors are consolidating, the massive logistics and food-packaging hubs in the GTA maintain a consistent need for box and carton makers.

National Trend: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) identifies this NOC as having a "Strong Risk of Shortage" due to expansion and high retirement rates (over 40% of the workforce is aged 50+).
Why Work in Canada as a Paper Converting Operator?
Sustainable Growth: You are part of the "Green Economy" shift, producing recyclable alternatives to plastic packaging.
Technological Shift: Transition from purely mechanical machines to operating digital interfaces and automated gluing/folding systems.
High Job Security: The essential nature of food and medical packaging ensures steady employment through economic cycles.
Advancement: Use this role as a foundation to move into Production Supervision (NOC 92014) or specialized Quality Control roles.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): A dedicated stream for temporary workers with 12 months of Canadian experience in essential manufacturing.
Ontario In-Demand Skills Stream: This NOC is frequently eligible for provincial nomination for those with a valid job offer in Ontario.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: An excellent route for semi-skilled workers in the manufacturing sector to gain Permanent Residency.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Many paper converting facilities are located in regional hubs where community-backed PR is a high-success option.
